title: quadlet: set a RuntimeDirectory= to allow system services with User=
body:
When using Quadlet to generate systemd system services setting `User=` results in permission errors creating files under `%t`:

```
Error: creating idfile: open /run/caddy.cid: permission denied
```

Setting [`RuntimeDirectory=%N`](https://www.freedesktop.org/software/systemd/man/latest/systemd.exec.html#RuntimeDirectory=) and moving the files to `%t/%N` results in podman having the required permissions to modify files:

> Use RuntimeDirectory= to manage one or more runtime directories for the unit and bind their lifetime to the daemon runtime. This is particularly useful for unprivileged daemons that cannot create runtime directories in /run/ due to lack of privileges, and to make sure the runtime directory is cleaned up automatically after use.

This allows running `.container` files with `User=`.

Example (`/etc/containers/systemd/caddy.container`):
```
[Service]
User=someuser
Group=someuser

[Container]
Exec=/usr/bin/caddy run
Image=docker.io/library/caddy

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```

`*.pod` files require further work due to restrictions on setting mainpid.


#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

```release-note
Fixed a bug where quadlet generated system services with `User=` failed with permission denied errors.
```

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **NOT APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *flaktack* **Once this PR has been reviewed and has the lgtm label**, please assign [baude](https://github.com/baude) for approval. For more information see [the Code Review Process](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process). The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman).
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - **[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)** Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

thanks for opening a PR, I don't think we can support this though.  Have you considered running it from the systemd user session?

Just running with `User=` and `Group=` is equivalent to running Podman through sudo as it does not create the systemd user session: https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md#31-podman-run-fails-with-erro0000-xdg_runtime_dir-directory-runuser0-is-not-owned-by-the-current-user-or-error-creating-tmpdir-mkdir-runuser1000-permission-denied  so we need at least a dependency on the user session
I agree running without user session is not really something we like to support or even encourage as it causes a lot of problems later on.

I'll expand a bit on my use case -- I realize that it is only enough to run containers with `User=*` in specific circumstances and there are other shortcomings that one can run into which may be reasons to avoid it.

Caddy is run as a systemd service with `User=*` for two reasons:
1. To allow using systemd socket activation with privileged ports (80, 443). 
2. To allow caddy access to a network and containers running in the user's session.

Due to the second item lingering mode is enabled for the user which may be why I didn't run into the linked issue.

For my use case this allows for rootless containers to be used with socket activation for privileged ports instead of using `ip_unprivileged_port_start` or other approaches.

> Due to the second item lingering mode is enabled for the user which may be why I didn't run into the linked issue.

But wouldn't this still race? The system service can be started before the user session is started. It would need to have explicit dependencies configured.

Another thing, wouldn't this break all already running containers services? Assume a contianer was started, then podman/quadlet was updated, then you systemctl daemon-reload which generates new unit files and now the ExecStop uses a new filepath that was not populated during start so all the stops/removals fail? Or does systemd somehow keep track of the ExecStop command that were defined one the units was originally started?

---

In any case I don't fully understand why we added the id files for qudalet in the first place, quadlet always assigns a default name so couldn't we just switch to only using the container/imagevolume/network/etc... names instead

cc @ygalblum
@Luap99 I think you are correct and it can be removed. I've opened a separate PR: https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/26007

Removing the `*.cid` file also "resolves" the file permission issue, so I'm closing this PR.

Yes, there probably is a race condition. I'll see if I can find something on that.
